network.overall_strategy: The network is organized around two major carbon-skeleton entry points established in the literature: (1) pyruvate as a central metabolic hub subject to reductive amination under diverse mineral/metal conditions, and (2) acetaldehyde as the Strecker-entry aldehyde for aminonitrile-first routes. These two hubs are interconnected by pyruvate decarboxylation (producing acetaldehyde), creating a cross-environment feedback loop. Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation builds C1→C2→C3 intermediates stepwise on greigite and FeNiS surfaces, generating pyruvate which then feeds multiple reductive amination pathways (Fe oxyhydroxide, Ni⁰, Ni⁰/pyridoxal, sulfide minerals). Surface environments provide Strecker chemistry (spark-discharge or hydrothermal-sourced acetaldehyde + HCN + NH₃), with formamide-stabilized aminonitrile variants. A proto-biochemical transamination module links glycine and pyruvate via metal-pyridoxal catalysis. All routes converge on DL-alanine, with a final chiral selection/amplification module (CPL photolysis, calcite adsorption, crystallization) generating L-alanine. The network explicitly acknowledges that homochirality is the least experimentally constrained step.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent fluid, pH 9-11, H₂ from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on the Fe₃S₄ surface which is structurally analogous to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. H₂ dissociates on the mineral surface providing hydride equivalents. Transfer of hydride to bound CO₂ yields formate (HCOO⁻), which desorbs into the alkaline vent fluid. This is the first committed step of an abiotic Wood-Ljungdahl-like pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is well-established as an analog of biological CO₂ fixation in the acetyl-CoA pathway. The structural similarity of Fe₃S₄ to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters has been noted by multiple groups. Thermodynamic analysis by Amend & Shock (1998, Science) shows amino acid synthesis from CO₂ is exergonic under hot, mildly reduced hydrothermal conditions. Serpentinization provides the H₂ reductant at alkaline vents.

reactions.name: Reductive carbonylation to acetyl thioester on FeNiS

conditions.other: pH 8-10, thiol/sulfide species present as acyl acceptors, analogous to acetyl-CoA pathway

reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced to CO (or a bound formyl intermediate) on FeNiS surfaces. A methyl group is built from sequential reduction of the C1 unit. CO insertion into the Fe/Ni-bound methyl yields an acetyl-metal intermediate, which is captured by thiolate to form an acetyl thioester. This is analogous to the biological acetyl-CoA synthase mechanism.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (1997) demonstrated CO-driven carbon-carbon bond formation on NiS/FeS under hydrothermal conditions, producing activated acetyl species. The acetyl-CoA pathway is considered one of the most ancient metabolic routes, and its abiotic analog on FeNiS minerals is a cornerstone of metabolism-first hypotheses. This step converts C1 (formate) to C2 (acetyl), the key chain-elongation event.

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation of acetyl thioester to pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 8-11, CO₂-rich vent fluid, analogous to reductive carboxylation in biological rTCA cycle

reactions.mechanism: The activated acetyl thioester undergoes carboxylation by CO₂ at the mineral surface. The carbonyl carbon of the thioester attacks CO₂ (or bicarbonate), forming a C3 β-keto acid intermediate that tautomerizes to pyruvate (2-oxopropanoate). This is analogous to the pyruvate synthase reaction in the reductive tricarboxylic acid cycle.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from C2 + CO₂ via reductive carboxylation is a key prediction of the iron-sulfur world hypothesis. While complete demonstrations of the full CO₂→pyruvate abiotic sequence remain limited, individual steps are well-supported. Novikov & Copley (2013, PNAS) used pyruvate as a starting point precisely because it is a plausible hydrothermal product at the C2-C3 junction. Amend & Shock (1998, Science) calculated that pyruvate synthesis is thermodynamically favored in hot reduced hydrothermal fluids (~−12 kJ/mol for alanine under specified conditions). This is the least experimentally constrained step in the network and should be considered an inference from thermodynamic and mechanistic plausibility rather than direct demonstration.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate on mixed-valence Fe oxyhydroxide

conditions.other: pH 10, completion in 48-72h, NH₃ in excess; best selectivity: ~70% alanine + ~30% lactate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and ammonia form a Schiff base (imine) intermediate at the mineral surface. The mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) oxyhydroxide provides reducing equivalents for the subsequent reduction of the imine to the amine (alanine). Competing reduction of the keto group directly produces lactate. The ratio of alanine:lactate depends on pH and Fe redox state. Purely ferrous hydroxide favors lactate; purely ferric gives no reaction; mixed valence at alkaline pH strongly favors alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (2019, PNAS) demonstrated maximum ~70% alanine yield (with ~30% lactate) from pyruvate + ammonia at pH 10, Fe(II):Fe(III)=1:1, 70°C, with completion over 48-72h. This is the highest-yield prebiotically plausible alanine synthesis reported. The mixed-valence iron phase mimics green-rust-like minerals that would form at redox/pH gradients in early Earth seafloor sediments and vent chimneys.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate on Ni⁰ nanoparticles

conditions.catalyst: Ni nanoparticles (or Pd nanoparticles at ~44% yield)
conditions.other: pH 7-8, 30 mM pyruvate, 24h; ~17% alanine yield on Ni, ~44% on Pd

reactions.mechanism: H₂ dissociatively adsorbs on the Ni⁰ surface, producing surface hydrides. Pyruvate and ammonia form a Schiff base in solution. The imine adsorbs on the metal surface and is reduced by surface hydrides to alanine. The mild conditions and high yields demonstrate that native metal phases can efficiently catalyze amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024, Chem) demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate (30 mM) + NH₃ at pH 7-8, 22°C, 24h, with H₂ at 5 bar, yielding ~17% alanine over Ni nanoparticles and ~44% over Pd nanoparticles. Native metals and metal alloys (e.g., awaruite Ni₃Fe) are produced during serpentinization and found in meteorites, making them plausible prebiotic catalysts.

reactions.name: Pyridoxal-enhanced reductive amination of pyruvate on Ni⁰

conditions.catalyst: Ni⁰ with pyridoxal as soluble cofactor
conditions.other: pH 11, 18h; 34% pyruvate→alanine conversion with pyridoxal vs 7.6% without; up to ~48% at higher temperatures. Pyridoxal suppresses competing lactate formation.

reactions.mechanism: Pyridoxal forms a Schiff base with ammonia, generating pyridoxamine (PMP analog). The pyridoxamine then transfers its amino group to pyruvate via a second Schiff base intermediate, regenerating pyridoxal and producing alanine. Ni⁰ catalyzes the initial pyridoxal + NH₃ + H₂ → pyridoxamine step, creating a catalytic nitrogen shuttle. This mimics biological transaminase chemistry but uses a mineral catalyst instead of an enzyme.
reactions.reasoning: Schlikker et al. (2024/2025, FEBS Journal) demonstrated that under serpentinization-like conditions (pH 11, 80°C, 5 bar H₂ on Ni⁰), pyridoxal addition increases pyruvate→alanine conversion from 7.6% to 34% (a 4.4× increase), with higher-temperature cases reaching ~48%. They also showed Ni⁰ catalyzes pyridoxal → pyridoxamine formation at ~5 mM NH₃. This represents a key bridge between mineral-tethered and soluble-cofactor-mediated amino acid synthesis.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate on transition metal sulfide minerals

conditions.other: 70 mM NH₄Cl, pyruvate at millimolar concentrations; alanine yields vary strongly with mineral type; competing pathways to lactate, propionate, and aldol products

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate adsorbs on sulfide mineral surfaces. NH₄⁺ provides nitrogen. The mineral surface mediates imine formation and reduction, but the dominant pathway depends strongly on which mineral is present. Different sulfide minerals 'prune' the pyruvate reaction landscape, selecting for different products (alanine, lactate, thiolation products, aldol products).
reactions.reasoning: Novikov & Copley (2013, PNAS) mapped pyruvate's reactivity landscape with several transition-metal sulfide minerals under vent-fluid-like gas loads (H₂/H₂S/CO₂/NH₄Cl). Reductive amination to alanine occurs, but at 'strikingly different levels' depending on the mineral present. This demonstrates that vent-wall mineral heterogeneity could generate diverse product distributions, with certain microenvironments favoring alanine.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Thermal decarboxylation is facile for β-keto and α-keto acids at hydrothermal temperatures; produces acetaldehyde + CO₂

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate (an α-keto acid) undergoes thermal decarboxylation at the mineral surface. The carboxylate group is lost as CO₂, and the resulting enol tautomerizes to acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO). This is a well-known thermal reaction that is accelerated by metal oxide surfaces and occurs readily above 300K.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde + CO₂ is a well-characterized reaction (350-400K) noted in the literature review as a key reaction type in biochemical/prebiotic environments. This reaction creates a critical cross-environment link: hydrothermal pyruvate can supply acetaldehyde to surface Strecker chemistry, making pyruvate a hub feeding both reductive amination and Strecker pathways.

reactions.name: Miller-type spark discharge producing Strecker feedstocks

conditions.other: Reducing gas mixture (CH₄, NH₃, H₂, H₂O vapor) exposed to continuous electrical discharge ~1 week; products accumulate in boiling water trap. Gas-phase radical chemistry generates HCN, aldehydes, and other reactive intermediates.

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reducing gas mixture generates reactive radical species (CH₃•, NH₂•, CN•, CHO•). Gas-phase radical recombination produces HCN, formaldehyde, and acetaldehyde among many products. These dissolve in the aqueous trap where they can undergo further solution-phase reactions including Strecker synthesis. This is a feedstock-generation step, not direct amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, Science) demonstrated production of amino acids from CH₄/NH₃/H₂/H₂O under electrical discharge. The mechanism was later understood as gas-phase radical chemistry producing HCN and aldehydes, followed by aqueous Strecker synthesis. Johnson et al. (2008, Science) showed that 'volcanic' spark configurations with steam aspiration yield broader amino acid inventories, with alanine/glycine molar ratios ranging from 0.027 to 1.4 depending on configuration. The TiO₂ agent is included as a photocatalytic mineral that may be present in volcanic aerosol environments and could participate in secondary photochemistry.

reactions.name: H₂S-enriched volcanic spark discharge to DL-alanine

conditions.other: Volcanic plume simulation; products include 23 amino acids and 4 amines; alanine detected racemic (D/L = 1 ± 10%)

reactions.mechanism: Similar radical chemistry to Miller-type discharge, but with H₂S and CO₂ replacing part of the reducing gas mixture. Sulfur radicals (HS•, S•) participate in radical recombination and may stabilize certain intermediates. The presence of H₂S shifts product distribution. Amino acids accumulate in the aqueous phase via in-situ Strecker-type aqueous chemistry from the discharge-generated HCN, aldehydes, and ammonia. The racemic nature of alanine (D/L ≈ 1) confirms abiotic synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. (2011, PNAS) analyzed archived samples from Miller's H₂S-enriched spark discharge experiments, identifying 23 amino acids including racemic alanine. The H₂S/CH₄/NH₃/CO₂ gas mixture simulates volcanic plume chemistry, which is particularly relevant if the early atmosphere was not globally reducing but had locally reducing volcanic environments. Sulfide mineral aerosols in volcanic settings could serve as additional catalytic surfaces.

reactions.name: Strecker condensation: acetaldehyde + ammonia + HCN → aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: clay surfaces concentrate reactants; alkaline pH favors imine formation
conditions.other: pH 8-10; acetaldehyde + NH₃ → imine (Schiff base), then HCN nucleophilic addition → aminonitrile; evaporitic pool or tidal flat environment

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base: CH₃CH=NH) with loss of water. Cyanide ion (CN⁻, from HCN at alkaline pH) attacks the electrophilic carbon of the imine in a nucleophilic addition, producing 2-aminopropionitrile (Ala-CN). The clay surface catalyzes the reaction by concentrating reactants in interlayer spaces and providing a mildly basic microenvironment.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the canonical prebiotic route to α-amino acids and has been invoked since Miller's original work. Diederich et al. (2023, PNAS) demonstrated alanine formation (180 µM, 0.02% yield based on NH₄Cl after 7 days) when acetaldehyde, KCN, and NH₄Cl are combined under alkaline conditions. The aminonitrile is the key intermediate; its formation is faster and more robust than its subsequent hydrolysis. Montmorillonite clay catalyzes concentration and provides a template surface for the condensation.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminopropionitrile to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: clay and mineral surfaces; mildly acidic to neutral pH accelerates hydrolysis
conditions.other: Hydrolysis proceeds via amide intermediate (2-aminopropanamide); wet-dry cycling can drive the reaction; this is often the rate-limiting step in Strecker alanine synthesis

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of 2-aminopropionitrile is hydrated to an amide (2-aminopropanamide) by nucleophilic attack of water, then the amide is hydrolyzed to the carboxylic acid (alanine). The overall conversion CN → CONH₂ → COOH requires two equivalents of water. Mineral surfaces catalyze the hydrolysis and wet-dry cycling concentrates the reactant while providing alternating conditions that favor different steps.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the second step of the Strecker synthesis and is widely considered the environmental bottleneck (Green et al. 2023, JACS; Diederich et al. 2023, PNAS). The hydrolysis is slow at neutral pH without catalysis, suggesting that mineral surfaces, elevated temperatures during dry phases, or UV-driven processes are necessary. Clay catalysis of nitrile hydrolysis has been documented in the prebiotic chemistry literature. The low overall yield (0.02% in Diederich et al.) may partly reflect this hydrolysis bottleneck.

reactions.name: Formamide-mediated stabilization of aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: Staged heating in formamide-rich pools; N-formylation is favored during drying/heating phases; N-formyl-Ala-CN becomes dominant product under extended heating

reactions.mechanism: In a formamide-rich environment (low water activity), the free amino group of 2-aminopropionitrile undergoes N-formylation by formamide, producing N-formyl-2-aminopropionitrile with release of ammonia. This protects the amine from side reactions and creates a more stable reservoir of the alanine precursor. The equilibrium between aminonitrile and N-formyl aminonitrile is controlled by temperature and water activity.
reactions.reasoning: Green et al. (2023, JACS) reported efficient formation of N-formyl-Ala-CN from acetaldehyde-derived inputs in formamide systems under staged heating. For the methyl-side-chain (alanine skeleton) case, N-formyl-Ala-CN becomes dominant after extended heating sequences. This provides a 'protected nitrile' route that stabilizes the alanine precursor pool, with later hydrolysis releasing free alanine under wetter conditions.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-formyl-aminopropionitrile to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces; wet phase of wet-dry cycles
conditions.other: Sequential hydrolysis: N-deformylation + nitrile hydrolysis; requires return to aqueous conditions after formamide-stabilized dry phase; pH 5-8

reactions.mechanism: Upon rewetting, the N-formyl group is hydrolyzed first (releasing formate), regenerating the free aminonitrile. The nitrile then undergoes standard hydrolysis (CN → CONH₂ → COOH) to produce DL-alanine. Alternatively, both hydrolyses can proceed in a concerted fashion under sufficiently warm, aqueous conditions. This step represents the 'release' phase of the protected-nitrile strategy.
reactions.reasoning: Green et al. (2023, JACS) established that formamide systems can drive aminonitrile ↔ N-formylaminonitrile partitioning. The subsequent hydrolysis to free amino acids is the release step, coupling dry-phase stabilization with wet-phase conversion. This addresses the 'precursor-to-persistent-pool' integration challenge identified in the literature: aminonitrile-first routes populate a precursor pool during dry phases and release amino acids during wet phases.

reactions.name: UV photolysis of HCN to formamide

conditions.catalyst: UV radiation (λ < 300 nm, strong on early Earth without ozone layer); TiO₂ photocatalysis
conditions.other: HCN + H₂O + UV → formamide; photolysis in shallow pools/tidal flats with UV exposure

reactions.mechanism: HCN in aqueous solution absorbs UV radiation (particularly around 200-260 nm). The excited HCN undergoes hydration to formamide (HCONH₂). TiO₂ mineral surfaces can act as photocatalysts, generating reactive oxygen species and facilitating the hydration. Formamide is a key prebiotic intermediate that feeds into both nucleobase and amino acid synthesis networks.
reactions.reasoning: UV photochemistry of HCN to produce formamide and subsequently nucleobases is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. On the early Earth without an ozone layer, strong UV flux at the surface would drive these transformations in shallow aqueous environments. TiO₂ photocatalysis further enhances these reactions. Formamide serves as both a solvent and reactant for the N-formylation route to protected aminonitriles (Green et al. 2023, JACS).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal NiS/CO-mediated synthesis of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: CO₂ reduced to CO; acetylene/C₂ intermediates formed on NiS surfaces and hydrated to acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is reduced to CO on iron-sulfide surfaces. CO reacts with H₂ on NiS to form C₂ intermediates (possibly via Fischer-Tropsch-type or acetylene intermediate chemistry). Hydration of the C₂ intermediate produces acetaldehyde. This provides a hydrothermal source of the key Strecker aldehyde that can be transported to surface environments.
reactions.reasoning: Diederich et al. (2023, PNAS) reported conditions under which acetaldehyde is generated from C₂H₂/CO/NiS-related chemistry, and when KCN and NH₄Cl are introduced under alkaline conditions, alanine forms (180 µM, 0.02% yield). This establishes a hydrothermal-to-surface pathway: vent chemistry produces acetaldehyde, which is then available for Strecker synthesis in surface pools receiving vent effluent.

reactions.name: UV photoreduction of CO₂ to formaldehyde on TiO₂

conditions.other: Shallow pools with mineral surfaces; anoxic early Earth atmosphere; CO₂ + 2H₂O + UV → HCHO + O₂ (O immediately consumed by reduced species)

reactions.mechanism: TiO₂ absorbs UV light, generating electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated electrons reduce adsorbed CO₂ via multiple proton-coupled electron transfer steps: CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde. The holes oxidize water or other reduced species. Under anoxic conditions, any oxidized products are rapidly scavenged by Fe²⁺ or H₂S.
reactions.reasoning: TiO₂-catalyzed photoreduction of CO₂ to formaldehyde is documented in photocatalysis literature and is prebiotically plausible given abundant TiO₂ minerals (anatase/rutile) and strong UV on the early Earth. Formaldehyde is a critical C1 feedstock for Strecker glycine synthesis, formose sugar chemistry, and other prebiotic networks.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of glycine from formaldehyde

conditions.other: HCHO + NH₃ → imine, then + HCN → aminoacetonitrile → hydrolysis → glycine; glycine is the simplest and most abundant amino acid in discharge experiments

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form methyleneimine (H₂C=NH). Cyanide addition gives aminoacetonitrile (glycine nitrile). Hydrolysis of the nitrile via amide intermediate yields glycine. This is the simplest Strecker synthesis and the most facile, consistent with glycine's typical dominance in prebiotic amino acid inventories.
reactions.reasoning: Glycine is the most abundant amino acid in Miller-type discharge experiments (Miller, 1953; Johnson et al., 2008) and its Strecker synthesis from formaldehyde + NH₃ + HCN is the simplest case. Glycine serves as an amino group donor for transamination reactions that can produce alanine from pyruvate (Dherbassy et al. 2023, JACS).

reactions.name: Ni⁰-catalyzed reductive amination of pyridoxal to pyridoxamine

conditions.other: pH 11, ~5 mM NH₃; serpentinization-like conditions; converts pyridoxal to pyridoxamine within hours, creating a nitrogen shuttle

reactions.mechanism: NH₃ condenses with the aldehyde group of pyridoxal to form a Schiff base (pyridoxal imine). H₂ dissociates on the Ni⁰ surface, and the surface hydrides reduce the imine to form pyridoxamine. This is the 'charging' step of the nitrogen shuttle: pyridoxamine can then transfer its amino group to keto acids.
reactions.reasoning: Schlikker et al. (2024/2025, FEBS Journal) demonstrated that Ni⁰ catalyzes pyridoxal → pyridoxamine formation with as little as ~5 mM NH₃ and H₂ (5 bar) at pH 11, 80°C within hours. This establishes that mineral catalysis can generate the 'charged' form of the B₆ cofactor scaffold under serpentinization-like conditions. NOTE: Pyridoxal itself is a complex heterocyclic molecule whose full prebiotic synthesis requires a separate network. Its inclusion here is justified by evidence from cyanosulfidic chemistry (Patel et al. 2015, Nature Chemistry) that complex heterocycles can form from shared prebiotic feedstocks (HCN, aldehydes, sulfur photochemistry), and by the conceptual necessity of bridging mineral-tethered and soluble-cofactor-mediated nitrogen chemistry.

reactions.name: Metal-pyridoxal catalyzed transamination: glycine + pyruvate → alanine

conditions.other: pH 5-7.5; Fe³⁺-pyridoxal at 75°C, 7.5 mol% loading is ~90× faster than pyridoxal alone and ~174× faster than Fe³⁺ alone; pyridoxal is catalytic (regenerated)

reactions.mechanism: Glycine forms a Schiff base with pyridoxal (PLP analog). The metal ion (Fe³⁺ or Al³⁺) chelates the Schiff base, stabilizing the planar intermediate and facilitating Cα-H bond cleavage. Tautomerization produces a ketimine intermediate, which hydrolyzes to release glyoxylate and pyridoxamine. Pyridoxamine then forms a Schiff base with pyruvate; analogous metal-assisted tautomerization and hydrolysis produce alanine and regenerate pyridoxal. The net reaction: glycine + pyruvate → alanine + glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Dherbassy et al. (2023, JACS) demonstrated strong metal-pyridoxal cooperativity in non-enzymatic transamination under prebiotically relevant conditions (20-75°C, pH 5-7.5). Fe³⁺-pyridoxal at 75°C is ~90× faster than pyridoxal alone and ~174× faster than Fe³⁺ alone. Al³⁺-pyridoxal shows similar cooperative acceleration. This represents a proto-biochemical route to alanine that bridges mineral/metal catalysis with soluble cofactor chemistry and demonstrates how enzymatic transamination may have emerged from simpler abiotic systems.

reactions.name: Chiral selection and amplification: DL-alanine → L-alanine enrichment

conditions.catalyst: chiral calcite surfaces (e.g., {2 1 3̄ 1} faces) and/or circularly polarized UV light
conditions.other: Multiple mechanisms operating in concert: (1) CPL photolysis of racemic alanine films yields up to ~4% optical purity; (2) enantioselective adsorption on chiral calcite (Hazen et al. 2001, but DFT shows <1 kcal/mol selectivity: Asthagiri & Hazen 2007); (3) enantioselective adsorption on homochiral quartz; (4) Viedma-type crystallization ripening for amplification of small ee. CRITICAL: this step is the acknowledged central bottleneck—small initial ee from any source must be amplified by iterative cycling.

reactions.mechanism: Three mechanisms can contribute to L-alanine enrichment from a racemic pool: (1) Circularly polarized UV light (from scattering off magnetized dust grains or reflection) preferentially destroys one enantiomer, generating up to ~4% ee in alanine films. (2) Chiral mineral surfaces (calcite, quartz) selectively adsorb one enantiomer, concentrating it on the surface while the other remains in solution. DFT calculations suggest <1 kcal/mol energy differences for D- vs L-alanine on chiral calcite. (3) Once any small ee exists, Viedma ripening (attrition-enhanced deracemization during crystallization) or Soai-type autocatalytic amplification could drive the system toward homochirality. The overall process likely requires repeated cycling through adsorption/desorption, crystallization/dissolution, or photolysis/recombination to achieve biologically relevant ee.
reactions.reasoning: Hazen et al. (2001, PNAS) reported enantioselective adsorption of amino acids on calcite, with D- and L-alanine showing selective behavior on different crystal faces. Asthagiri & Hazen (2007) calculated <1 kcal/mol energy differences by DFT, implying only modest equilibrium selectivity. CPL photolysis experiments (reported in Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres) show up to ~4% optical purity for alanine. Enantioselective adsorption on homochiral quartz has also been tested. The literature is explicit that strong ee for alanine likely requires amplification mechanisms (repeated cycling, crystallization dynamics) because the primary synthesis routes are racemic (Parker et al. 2011: D/L = 1 ± 10%) and single-pass adsorption selectivity is weak. This remains the central open question in L-alanine prebiotic chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation → Fe oxyhydroxide reductive amination
pathways.description: Complete hydrothermal pathway from CO₂ to L-alanine: stepwise CO₂ reduction to formate, then to acetyl thioester, then reductive carboxylation to pyruvate, followed by reductive amination on mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) oxyhydroxide (Barge et al. 2019, ~70% yield at optimal conditions), and final chiral selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation → Ni⁰ reductive amination
pathways.description: Same CO₂ fixation as P1, but pyruvate is reduced to alanine by Ni⁰ nanoparticles under mild aqueous conditions with H₂ (Kaur et al. 2024, ~17% on Ni, ~44% on Pd). Represents meteoritic/serpentinite native metal catalysis.

pathways.name: Pyridoxal-enhanced serpentinization pathway
pathways.description: Ni⁰ catalyzes both pyridoxal→pyridoxamine (nitrogen shuttle charging) and the subsequent pyridoxal-enhanced reductive amination of pyruvate (Schlikker et al. 2024/2025, 34% yield with pyridoxal vs 7.6% without). Represents the bridge from mineral to cofactor-mediated chemistry under serpentinization conditions.

pathways.name: Spark discharge → classical Strecker synthesis
pathways.description: Miller-type spark discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde feedstocks (Miller 1953; Johnson et al. 2008), which undergo classical Strecker condensation in aqueous pools to form aminopropionitrile, followed by hydrolysis to DL-alanine. The most historically established route.

pathways.name: Spark discharge → formamide-stabilized Strecker
pathways.description: HCN from spark discharge is photolyzed to formamide (UV, TiO₂). Acetaldehyde + NH₃ + HCN form aminopropionitrile, which is N-formylated in formamide-rich dry pools (Green et al. 2023). The protected nitrile is stored and later hydrolyzed to alanine during wet phases. Provides a precursor-pool stabilization mechanism.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde → surface Strecker (cross-environment)
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde is generated hydrothermally via NiS/CO chemistry (Diederich et al. 2023), then transported to surface pools where it undergoes Strecker synthesis with surface-environment HCN and NH₃. Represents a critical hydrothermal→surface material flow.

pathways.name: H₂S volcanic discharge direct pathway
pathways.description: Sulfur-enriched volcanic spark discharge directly produces racemic alanine among 23 amino acids (Parker et al. 2011). The entire gas-phase radical chemistry and aqueous Strecker processing occurs in situ. Product is racemic (D/L = 1 ± 10%). Simplest overall pathway.

pathways.name: Proto-biochemical transamination (glycine + pyruvate)
pathways.description: Glycine from formaldehyde-Strecker (surface) and pyruvate from hydrothermal fixation converge in a metal-pyridoxal catalyzed transamination (Dherbassy et al. 2023, Fe³⁺-pyridoxal ~90× faster than pyridoxal alone). This is the most 'biochemically-looking' route, bridging two environments and demonstrating how enzymatic amino transfer could have emerged.

pathways.name: Sulfide mineral vent pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate from hydrothermal CO₂ fixation undergoes reductive amination in the presence of diverse sulfide minerals and vent gases (H₂/H₂S/CO₂). Different minerals 'prune' the reaction landscape differently (Novikov & Copley 2013). Represents the mineral-diversity-dependent pathway.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation → acetaldehyde → Strecker (cross-environment feedback)
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate undergoes thermal decarboxylation to acetaldehyde (+ CO₂), creating a C3→C2 cross-link. This acetaldehyde then enters surface Strecker chemistry with HCN and NH₃, regenerating alanine via the aminonitrile route. This creates a feedback loop: pyruvate hub feeds both reductive amination AND Strecker pathways, demonstrating network redundancy.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong pathway combining well-supported Fe oxyhydroxide reductive amination (Barge et al. 2019, up to ~70% yield) with more speculative early CO₂ fixation steps. The formate→acetyl thioester→pyruvate chain (rxn_002/003) draws on iron-sulfur world proposals but is less experimentally constrained than the downstream amination. Chiral selection step is the weakest link. The pathway properly flows Hydrothermal→Biochemical.
suggestions: Provide more experimental support for reductive carboxylation of acetyl thioester to pyruvate
Consider specifying the chiral selection mechanism more concretely with expected ee values

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nearly identical to P1 but uses Ni⁰ nanoparticle catalysis for reductive amination, well-supported by Kaur et al. 2024 (~17% yield on Ni, ~44% on Pd at pH 7-8, 22°C, 24h). Same upstream limitations as P1. Provides good redundancy to the Fe oxyhydroxide route.
suggestions: Consider including Pd as an alternative catalyst option given its higher reported yields

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-aligned with Schlikker et al. 2024/2025 showing pyridoxal boosts pyruvate→alanine from 7.6% to 34% on Ni⁰ at pH 11, 80°C. However, pyridoxal (mol_019) has no prebiotic synthesis route defined in this network—it appears as an intermediate but is never produced. This is a significant gap since pyridoxal is a complex molecule. The serpentinization framing is appropriate.
suggestions: Add a reaction or note explaining prebiotic availability of pyridoxal
Consider whether pyridoxal could be generated from simpler network precursors

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic and well-established Strecker pathway. Spark discharge producing feedstocks is supported by Miller 1953 and Johnson et al. 2008. Strecker condensation of acetaldehyde+NH₃+HCN is textbook prebiotic chemistry. Hydrolysis of aminonitrile to alanine is plausible but can be slow at neutral pH (literature notes this as a bottleneck). Environmental progression Surface→Biochemical is clean. Only 4 steps, making it one of the most parsimonious routes.
suggestions: Specify conditions/catalysts that could accelerate aminonitrile hydrolysis prebiotically

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formamide-stabilized variant supported by Green et al. 2023 JACS showing N-formyl-Ala-CN formation. The HCN→formamide step (rxn_015) is described as 'UV photolysis' which is somewhat nonstandard—HCN hydration to formamide is more conventional. The stabilization concept is valuable and addresses the hydrolysis timing bottleneck identified in the literature. Adds useful complexity to the simpler P4 route.
suggestions: Clarify the HCN→formamide mechanism: hydration vs photolysis
Consider whether formamide-rich microenvironments are geochemically sustainable

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent cross-environment pathway: hydrothermal acetaldehyde production (supported by Diederich et al. 2023 NiS/CO chemistry) feeding into surface Strecker synthesis. This Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical flow is exactly the kind of environmental interconnection the framework calls for. The acetaldehyde bridge between environments is chemically justified. Compact 4-step route.
suggestions: Discuss transport mechanism for acetaldehyde from hydrothermal to surface environment
Note acetaldehyde's volatility as both an advantage (transport) and challenge (loss)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Most direct route, only 2 steps. Parker et al. 2011 demonstrated 23 amino acids from H₂S-enriched volcanic discharge, with alanine racemic within ±10%. However, this pathway essentially treats the entire synthesis as a black box (single discharge reaction). While experimentally validated, it offers limited mechanistic insight and the Surface→Biochemical progression is minimal. The directness is both a strength (parsimony) and weakness (no intermediate control points).
suggestions: Consider breaking rxn_010 into sub-steps for mechanistic transparency
Add intermediate Strecker steps that likely occur within the discharge system

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Impressive proto-biochemical pathway spanning all three environments: Hydrothermal (pyruvate synthesis), Surface (glycine via formaldehyde Strecker + UV photoreduction on TiO₂), Biochemical (metal-pyridoxal transamination). Supported by Dherbassy et al. 2023 showing Fe³⁺-pyridoxal is ~90× faster than pyridoxal alone. However, same pyridoxal sourcing issue as P3, and the pathway requires convergence of three independently generated molecules (pyruvate, glycine, pyridoxal). The 7-step length adds cumulative yield concerns.
suggestions: Discuss concentration/compartmentalization requirements for three-component convergence

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Fully hydrothermal vent pathway using sulfide minerals, well-aligned with Novikov & Copley 2013's pyruvate reactivity landscape. The literature emphasizes that yields vary 'strikingly' across different minerals, adding realism but also uncertainty. Same upstream CO₂ fixation concerns. Provides diversity in mineral catalysts compared to P1/P2.
suggestions: Specify which sulfide minerals are most favorable for alanine selectivity based on Novikov & Copley data
Note the competition with lactate and propionate formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The most network-aware pathway, creating the cross-environment feedback loop (pyruvate decarboxylation → acetaldehyde → Strecker). This demonstrates the interconnected nature of the network—hydrothermal carbon fixation generates pyruvate, which can either directly undergo reductive amination OR decarboxylate to acetaldehyde for surface Strecker chemistry. The decarboxylation step is thermally facile. This pathway beautifully illustrates the convergence of metabolism-first and Strecker-first paradigms. The Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical flow is well-justified.
suggestions: Discuss whether this feedback loop could be self-sustaining
Consider kinetic competition between direct reductive amination vs decarboxylation

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an exceptionally well-designed prebiotic synthesis network for L-alanine that demonstrates deep engagement with the literature. The dual-hub strategy around pyruvate (metabolism-first) and acetaldehyde (Strecker-first) accurately reflects the field's consensus on alanine's major prebiotic entry points. Nearly every reaction maps onto specific experimental literature: Barge et al. 2019 (Fe oxyhydroxide), Kaur et al. 2024 (Ni⁰), Schlikker et al. 2024/2025 (pyridoxal/Ni⁰), Parker et al. 2011 (volcanic discharge), Green et al. 2023 (formamide stabilization), Diederich et al. 2023 (NiS acetaldehyde), Novikov & Copley 2013 (sulfide minerals), and Dherbassy et al. 2023 (metal-pyridoxal transamination). The network topology is rich with 5 genuine hub molecules, 10 distinct pathways, and meaningful cross-environment connections. Three notable weaknesses: (1) The chiral selection step (rxn_021) is the acknowledged Achilles heel—literature supports only ~4% ee from CPL and <1 kcal/mol adsorption differences on calcite, making the DL→L step the least experimentally constrained. (2) The early carbon fixation chain (rxn_001→002→003) producing pyruvate from CO₂ is mechanistically plausible but has limited direct experimental demonstration at the yields needed. (3) Pyridoxal (mol_019) appears as an intermediate in P3, P8 without a defined prebiotic synthesis route within the network. Despite these issues, the network's redundancy, literature alignment, mineral specification, and environmental flow make it a high-quality representation of current prebiotic alanine chemistry knowledge.
